content.problemStatement:
Chef loves experimenting with numbers and powers. Today, Chef wants to create a function to calculate the power of a given basebasebase raised to an integer exponentexponentexponent. This exponentexponentexponent can be positive, negative, or zero.

Chef needs to handle cases where the basebasebase is a floating-point number and the exponentexponentexponent is a large integer, ensuring accurate results even for fractional and negative powers.

Help Chef implement this power calculation correctly and efficiently!

Function Declaration

**Function Name**



calculatePowercalculatePowercalculatePower



**Parameters**



basebasebase (double): The base value to be raised to a power.
exponentexponentexponent (long long): This indicates the power to which the base is raised.



**Return Value**



Returns a double representing the computed power value.
The output is the result of basebasebase raised to the exponentexponentexponent power.
Handles fractional and negative exponents correctly, returning a floating-point number.


Input Format
The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
Each of the next TTT lines contains two space-separated values:

A double basebasebase — the base number.
An integer exponentexponentexponent — the power to which the base is raised.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing a double — the result of baseexponentbase^{exponent}baseexponent.
The result should be accurate up to at least 5 decimal places.
Constraints
1≤T≤1001 \leq T \leq 1001≤T≤100
−100.0<base<100.0-100.0 < base < 100.0−100.0<base<100.0
−231≤exponent≤231−1-2^{31} \leq exponent \leq 2^{31} - 1−231≤exponent≤231−1
exponentexponentexponent is an integer.
Either base≠0base \neq 0base=0 or exponent>0exponent > 0exponent>0.
−104≤baseexponent≤104-10^{4} \leq base^{exponent} \leq 10^{4}−104≤baseexponent≤104
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated values: a double base and a 64‑bit signed integer exponent.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line with the value base^exponent printed with exactly five digits after the decimal point.

sampleTestCases.explanation: 99.99 raised to 1 is 99.99. -99.99 raised to 1 is -99.99.

hidden.label: negative base with negative odd exponent

solution.approach: Use binary (fast) exponentiation. If exponent is zero, answer is 1. If exponent is negative, replace base with its reciprocal and work with the absolute value of exponent. Repeatedly square the base and multiply the result when the current exponent bit is 1. This runs in O(log |exponent|) time and O(1) extra space.
